title: The Mindblower Prize: Rs 50,000 for Mind-Blowing Ideas
text:
The Mindblower Prize: Rs 50,000 for Mind-Blowing Ideas
decisions
Nitin Pai is offering up to three prizes of Rs 50,000 each to staff who present truly original, mind-blowing ideas.
Published
June 29, 2026
TL;DR
: Nitin Pai is offering up to three prizes of Rs 50,000 each to staff who present truly original, mind-blowing ideas.
Key Points
Up to three prizes of Rs 50,000 each are available
The sole judging criterion is whether the idea blows Nitin Pai's mind
Ideas can be new concepts, new ways of thinking, or new solutions to problems
Submissions must address first-level and some second-level questions, but need not be fully developed
Open to all staff, including interns
Prizes will be presented at tankscrub and other opportunities
Decisions Made
✅ Nitin Pai established the Mindblower Prize with up to three awards of Rs 50,000 each
✅ Prize is open to all Takshashila staff including interns
Context
Takshashila Institution regularly hosts 'tankscrub,' an internal event where ideas and research are shared and critiqued. The Mindblower Prize is an incentive introduced by Nitin Pai (likely the head of Takshashila) to encourage creative and original thinking among staff at all levels, rewarding intellectual boldness rather than fully polished work.
Original Thread
[2024-07-02 05:53 UTC] @nitinpai: **The Mindblower Prize**

Up to three Prizes of Rs 50,000 each to folks who blow *my* mind with an idea or concept; presented at tankscrub (but also at other opportunities).

The criteria is simple: it should blow *my* mind. A new idea, a new way of thinking, a new solution to a problem etc. It must be sufficiently thought out to answer first level questions, and perhaps some second level questions. But it need not be fully baked.

Open to all staff, including interns.

title: Who We Are — Takshashila Profile

Who We Are — Takshashila Profile
notes
What Takshashila is, what it does, and how it is organised.
Published
April 29, 2026
TL;DR
: Takshashila is an independent think tank and graduate school of public policy based in Bengaluru, founded in 2010, working at the intersection of research, education, and public discourse.
Key Points
Independent think tank and graduate school of public policy, Bengaluru, est. 2010
Works on international relations, defence, national security, and technology policy
Three pillars: research, education (Policy School), and public discourse (podcast, newsletters, journal)
Engages with PMO, Ministry of Defence, and international policy dialogues
Alumni networks across multiple continents
Research Circles
Seven active research programmes:
Indo-Pacific Studies
High-Tech Geopolitics
Advanced Biology
Economic Policy
Strategic Studies
Advanced Military Technologies
Geospatial Research
Policy School
Founded 2012. Unique model: students study while maintaining employment. Flagship offering is the Graduate Certificate in Public Policy (GCPP) — 12 weeks, three specialisations:
Defence & Foreign Affairs
Tech & Policy
Advanced Public Policy
Also offers a year-long Post Graduate Programme (PGP) and shorter specialised courses.
Public Outputs
All Things Policy
— daily podcast on public policy
Indian Public Policy Review
— peer-reviewed, open-access journal
Multiple newsletters
OpenTakshashila
— community platform for policy discussions

title: Mission, Values & Code of Conduct
text:
Mission, Values & Code of Conduct
decisions
Takshashila's founding mission, core values, and the code of conduct all staff are expected to embody.
Published
April 29, 2026
TL;DR
: Takshashila exists to advance freedom, openness, tolerance, pluralism, and responsible citizenship — and to strengthen India through better public policy and governance.
Mission
An independent centre for research and education in public policy. Non-partisan, non-profit. Dedicated to advancing
freedom, openness, tolerance, pluralism, and responsible citizenship
.
Drives change by uniting capable individuals with quality ideas and robust networks.
On Values (Nitin Pai)
Long-term continuity over growth-focused exits
Excellence is the primary observable measure of values
Diversity across multiple dimensions
Pro-market, not pro-business
Non-partisanship ≠ neutrality — policies and their consequences define values
Institutional survival is the biggest moral imperative
The lighthouse logo is the symbol: a beacon for these principles
Code of Conduct
Integrity
No plagiarism
Admit knowledge gaps openly
Disclose conflicts of interest
Maintain flexible hypotheses — update when evidence demands
Prioritise institutional goals and collaborative synergy over personal visibility
Conduct
Decency in all discourse
Disengage when discussions escalate unproductively
Mindful language and cultural sensitivity
Elevating Policy Debate
Recognise and credit original thought
Adopt interdisciplinary approaches
Judge policies by their human consequences, not political alignment
Independence
Maintain a clear personal-vs-institutional distinction; always use explicit disclaimers when expressing personal views
Prioritise India's national interests in framing
Key Reference
The Year Zero report at
takshashila.org.in/year-zero
provides foundational context on institutional values.

title: How We Think — Critical Thinking Norms
text:
How We Think — Critical Thinking Norms
playbook
The frameworks and principles Takshashila staff use to reason rigorously about policy questions.
Published
April 29, 2026
TL;DR
: Takshashila's thinking norms: come with three alternatives not just a problem, lead with conclusions, quantify claims, hold hypotheses lightly, and apply Occam's Razor.
Three Cardinal Rules
Prepare before seeking help
— come with at least three pre-considered options, not just a problem statement
Solution-oriented communication
— accompany any publicly raised issue with proposed solutions
Action over permission
— better to apologise for acting than to wait indefinitely for approval
Baloney Detection (Michael Shermer)
Before asserting a claim, check:
Is the source reliable and independent?
Has the claim been verified independently?
Does it align with what is otherwise known?
Does the evidence support this claim over alternatives?
Sagan's Method
Seek independent confirmation of facts
Encourage debate among experts — do not appeal to authority
Test multiple hypotheses simultaneously
Quantify
— if something can be measured, measure it; it allows better discrimination between competing ideas
Do not become attached to your own hypothesis
Every link in an argument chain must be sound
Apply Occam's Razor: prefer the simpler explanation when evidence is equal
Hypotheses must be falsifiable — unfalsifiable claims are not scientific
The Reverse Bollywood Format
In communication and presentations:
lead with the conclusion
. State the key point or recommendation in 60 seconds. Methodology and evidence follow. Never build up to a reveal.

title: The Flag System — Gold, Red, Yellow, White
text:
The Flag System — Gold, Red, Yellow, White
playbook
How Takshashila uses four flags to signal achievements, blockers, and accountability in day-to-day work.
Published
April 29, 2026
TL;DR
: Four flags — Gold (win), Red (urgent blocker), Yellow (stuck after asking), White (owning a mistake) — are the shared language for signalling work status at Takshashila.
Key Points
Flags are raised in meetings and on Mattermost, not just kept private
Gold flags feed into stakeholder reports and performance reviews — raise them
Don't wait for a meeting to raise a Red flag; do it immediately
White flags are encouraged, not penalised — mistakes are steps toward growth
The Four Flags
🟡 Gold Flag — Achievements
Claim when an achievement is unlocked or when you want to share something that made you happy or proud.
Use for: publications, successful events, closed grants, positive stakeholder feedback, course completions.
Also feeds into stakeholder reporting and performance reviews — log them.
🔴 Red Flag — Urgent Blocker
Raise when your progress is blocked, there is a crisis, and you need immediate help and attention from colleagues.
Do not wait for the next meeting.
Post on Mattermost right away.
🟠 Yellow Flag — Persistent Blocker
Raise when you have already asked for help and are still stuck. Escalates the red flag to the whole team.
🏳️ White Flag — Owning a Mistake
Raise to own up to mistakes and oversights.
The organisation actively encourages white flags. Surfacing mistakes quickly allows collective course-correction. Hiding them does not.
Where Flags Are Raised
Weekly Ops Meeting
(Tuesdays 11:30am) — standard venue for flags
Mattermost
— for red flags that can't wait

title: Tankscrub — Philosophy & Format
text:
Tankscrub — Philosophy & Format
playbook
What Tankscrub is, why it exists, and how to participate well.
Published
April 29, 2026
TL;DR
: Tankscrub is a Thursday morning gathering for half-baked ideas and organisational curiosity — deliberately informal, self-deprecating in tone, and the opposite of a formal presentation.
Key Points
Informal by design — resist the urge to make it a polished presentation
Share preliminary thoughts with intellectual rigour but without unnecessary formality
The tone should be irreverent and self-deprecating
Keeps the team's curiosity and learning radars active
The Philosophy (Nitin Pai)
"Our work is too serious for seriousness."
Tankscrub exists to keep oldtimers on their toes and ensure curiosity never stagnates. The common pitfall is treating it as a formal presentation venue — don't.
How to Start a Tankscrub Discussion
Three suggested conversation openers:
"A few days ago, I was reading X and it struck me that Y"
"D says X, and I think Y evidence supports it, but I think Z"
"The Z government's recent proposal P can lead to C consequences"
Thursday Thali
One person or group arranges monthly lunch for the office (~₹250 per plate). Develops negotiation skills and camaraderie. The SOP: select cuisine → confirm logistics → announce preferences → collect counts → coordinate delivery → submit for reimbursement.

TL;DR

✅ Laptop reimbursement available after 6-month probation, spread over 24 months via payroll

POSH Policy
All employees have the right to work in an environment free of sexual or other harassment. The policy applies beyond office hours to all work-related events including conferences and business trips. Consensual relationships between colleagues are not considered harassment under this policy.
Annual POSH training is mandatory
for all staff.

Consult the Leave Policy document (available on the Resources page of the staff handbook) before submitting any leave request.
Confidentiality
Unreleased materials — research drafts, internal documents, strategy papers — must not be shared externally without explicit permission from your supervisor. Employee contracts detail intellectual property classifications.
Skill Development
Takshashila reimburses up to

for courses in soft skills, project management, multimedia editing, or languages. Conditions: - Manager approval required before enrolment - Assigned work must be completed before reimbursement is processed
Laptop Policy

1. Select your preferred device (Macs recommended) 2. Email your manager for approval, copying Shiva Kumar 3. Purchase the device 4. Submit receipt to Shiva Kumar 5. Reimbursement spreads across 24 months via payroll

One-time benefit. Reimbursement ceases if you leave the organisation.

title: Low Course Uptake Attributed to Economy and Oversupply
text:
Low Course Uptake Attributed to Economy and Oversupply
decisions
Recent Takshashila courses are seeing low enrollment, and the team is investigating whether macroeconomic conditions or internal scheduling issues are to blame.
Published
June 20, 2026
TL;DR
: Recent Takshashila courses are seeing low enrollment, and the team is investigating whether macroeconomic conditions or internal scheduling issues are to blame.
Key Points
Low uptake observed for West Asia (Raja), China Defence (Anushka), and Geospatial (Nithiya) courses.
Hypothesis 1: Broader economic slowdown and hiring pauses are causing learners to deprioritize courses outside their immediate domain.
Hypothesis 2: Takshashila may be offering too many courses simultaneously, or scheduling overlaps create thematic competition.
AI and Critical Minerals courses bucked the trend, likely due to direct relevance to learners' work.
A review will be conducted after three months to assess the situation.
Decisions Made
✅ Team will revisit course uptake data after three months.
✅ Malathi will propose the way forward for Executive Certificate Courses (ECCs).
Action Items
Malathi to propose the way ahead for ECCs (timeline: review in ~3 months from June 2026).
Context
Takshashila offers a range of Executive Certificate Courses (ECCs) on topics spanning geopolitics, defence, technology, and energy. This conversation took place in a recurring internal sync ('Sesame') where course performance and programming decisions are discussed. The pattern of low enrollment across multiple courses prompted a broader conversation about external and internal factors affecting demand.
Original Thread
[2026-06-09 06:23 UTC] @pranaykotas: One thing from today's sesame:

The uptake for all recent courses seems to be quite low. This includes the West Asia course by Raja, China Defence by Anushka, and Geospatial course by Nithiya. One hypothesis that came up was that this is a reflection of the broader economy.

With hiring pauses, people might be cutting on expenditure that is not related to their domain. We still had reasonably good numbers for the AI course (possibly because it's related to their work) and for the critical minerals course (from the energy transition crowd).

The other hypothesis was that there is oversupply of courses from our end or scheduling overlaps from a thematic angle.

We decided to take a look again after three months and @malathi will be proposing the way ahead for ECCs.

title: Do Not Resolve or Delete Comments When Sharing Docs for Review
text:
Do Not Resolve or Delete Comments When Sharing Docs for Review
playbook
Deleting reviewer comments frustrates collaborators — always respond to comments with your reasoning instead of resolving or removing them.
Published
June 16, 2026
TL;DR
: Deleting reviewer comments frustrates collaborators — always respond to comments with your reasoning instead of resolving or removing them.
Key Points
Never resolve or delete comments on a Google Doc sent for review.
Respond to each comment with the action taken — whether the change was made or why it was not.
Deleting reviewer comments is considered disrespectful and frustrating to collaborators.
This practice maintains a clear audit trail of feedback and editorial decisions.
Context
At Takshashila, research documents are frequently shared internally for peer review before publication. This norm was flagged to ensure that all researchers and writers follow a consistent, respectful commenting etiquette on collaborative documents, particularly Google Docs.
Original Thread
[2026-06-16 15:00 UTC] @pranaykotas: an important practice to follow in research. When you send a google doc for review to anyone, DO NOT resolve or delete comments. You can just respond to them with the changes you have made or chosen not to make. It makes a reviewer super angry to review documents when their previous comments are deleted.

title: AIForward Strategy: Balancing Speed, Quality, and Human Development
text:
AIForward Strategy: Balancing Speed, Quality, and Human Development
decisions
Takshashila is implementing an AIForward approach that emphasizes rapid iteration, hiring high-capability people, maintaining quality through internal friction, and shifting toward idea promotion over pure output.
Published
June 2, 2026
TL;DR
: Takshashila is implementing an AIForward approach that emphasizes rapid iteration, hiring high-capability people, maintaining quality through internal friction, and shifting toward idea promotion over pure output.
Key Points
Rapid iteration needed for initial hypothesis and middle cycles, with more deliberate approach to final stage outputs
Hiring strategy targets 'first quadrant' candidates with both high AIQ and human IQ
Quality control through 'internal friction': handwritten work in first year, mandatory circle head reviews for op-eds, compulsory seminars for research
Performance evaluation shift toward 'warrior' mindset focused on persuasion and idea promotion rather than just scholarly output
Decisions Made
✅ Implement AIForward strategy with emphasis on rapid early/middle cycles
✅ Require all new hires to handwrite everything in their first year
✅ Make circle head approval mandatory for all op-eds
✅ Make seminars compulsory for research documents
✅ Include persuasion and idea promotion in performance appraisals
Context
This appears to be strategic guidance from leadership about how Takshashila should adapt its research and operational processes in an AI-enabled environment. The discussion reflects concerns about maintaining quality and human development while leveraging AI capabilities for faster output cycles.
Original Thread
[2026-06-02 05:34 UTC] @pranaykotas: 1. Focus on getting earlier cycles of outputs rapidly (initial hypothesis). Middle cycles also need to be done rapidly. We need to think through more about the final stage.
2. We want people in first quadrant (high AIQ, high Human IQ)
3. Since we care for our people, we need to put in internal friction. Handwrite everything in the first year. No op-ed goes without running the argument by circle heads. Seminars are compulsory for research documents.
4. AIForward implies spending more time on persuasion and sales of your idea instead of only writing more outputs. More of a warrior rather than a scholar. Need to introduce that into our appraisal

[2025-11-20 09:40 UTC] @nitinpai: Here's a draft of a Research Competency Level Framework. Please comment/suggest edits.

We should put this out to enable staff to make these transitions.

### Level 1 - Observer
What you do: follow current developments through multiple sources and viewpoints, and have up-to-minute information on current affairs related to the field. Follow the work of key analysts and scholars. This includes knowledge of key players, their interests, motivations and goals; dynamics between players; accurate facts and figures at the fingertips

What you should be able to do: write & present accurate & up-to-minute briefs, provide research assistance in terms of finding sources, correct citation. Assist in teaching specific classes in graduate programmes.

### Level 2 - Analyst
What you do: be familiar with the latest developments in the field, be familiar with developments in adjacent fields, be familiar with the work of other analysts, be able to connect dots and discern patterns, be familiar with models of the world and how they work.

What you should be able to do: prepare and present assessments and creative policy options; teach a full course module in a graduate programme independently

### Level 3 - Expert
What you do: Be familiar with all important analytical perspectives in the field, understand limitations of current knowledge, understand stakeholder interests, suggest policy recommendations, look out & nurture breakthrough ideas

What you should be able to do: develop theories and conceptual frameworks to explain phenomena and help predict outcomes, make policy recommendations, develop complete courses in graduate programmes

title: WhatsApp Group Discussion Strategy: Handling Opposing Views
text:
WhatsApp Group Discussion Strategy: Handling Opposing Views
playbook
A strategic approach to engaging with misinformation and opposing viewpoints in WhatsApp groups while maintaining credibility and civility.
Published
May 13, 2026
TL;DR
: A strategic approach to engaging with misinformation and opposing viewpoints in WhatsApp groups while maintaining credibility and civility.
Key Points
Make polite interventions by pointing out factual errors or logical flaws to signal alternative viewpoints exist
Use Socratic questioning to challenge claims (e.g., 'Where in the Arthashastra does it say so?')
Keep interventions brief - one or two messages maximum, avoid long arguments
Choose battles wisely and maintain credibility by praising what's good while presenting fact-based contrary views
The goal is exposure of counter-views, not winning debates or changing minds immediately
Context
This conversation emerged from the common challenge of navigating social media discussions where misinformation or partisan views dominate. Given Takshashila's focus on evidence-based policy discourse, this represents a practical guide for public intellectuals and policy practitioners engaging in online discussions.
Original Thread
[2024-01-25 11:30 UTC] @nitinpai: Sharing this here because quite a number of people are facing this.

How do you handle WhatsApp groups, where a lot of people believe in things that you oppose.

1. Make a polite intervention, pointing out why something is not based on fact, or why the argument is not based on logic etc. This is to signal to everyone that there is a different point of view, and that the majority view has been challenged.

2. One way to do the above is to ask questions. Eg "Where in the Arthashastra does it say so?"

3. Do not get into a long argument. One or two messages are enough. You can't 'win' this debate by debating.

4. Chill. Get on with your life.

The purpose is to let others know that a counter view exists.

[2024-01-25 11:59 UTC] @yusuf: Sounds like my average day on teetar.

I think it's important to make our views heard in developing opinion. If one is overtly and overly partisan, you'd not be heard and dismissed. The way to go about it is to praise what's good, and lay out arguments as Nitin stated above when you want to present a contrary view based on facts, data etc.

Also, chose your battles, err I mean topics.

title: Adopting Universalism in Research Culture: Critique Without Power Dynamics
text:
Adopting Universalism in Research Culture: Critique Without Power Dynamics
playbook
Takshashila should embrace a research culture where ideas are critiqued based on merit alone, not the identity or status of who proposes them.
Published
April 30, 2026
TL;DR
: Takshashila should embrace a research culture where ideas are critiqued based on merit alone, not the identity or status of who proposes them.
Key Points
Research institutions should critique ideas universally, not shield scholars based on power dynamics or identity
Avoiding criticism of junior or marginalized researchers can be infantilizing and exclude them from full participation in science
Modern power dynamics are multidimensional—junior researchers can have significant online influence
Critique should be rigorous but delivered with care, distinguishing between idea criticism and personal attacks
Takshashila's identity as a research establishment, not conventional corporate, supports adopting this culture
Decisions Made
✅ Takshashila should adopt a corporate culture based on universal critique of ideas regardless of author identity or status
Context
This conversation arose from leadership reflecting on organizational culture and research standards at Takshashila Institution. The discussion was prompted by an academic essay about the problems with 'punching down' norms in academia and the need for universal standards in scholarly critique.
Links & References
Michael Inzlicht's essay on universalism and critique in research
Original Thread
[2026-04-30 03:57 UTC] @nitinpai: Please go through [this post](https://x.com/minzlicht/status/2049492221360025765). A lot of people get carried away by social norms. We are not a conventional corporate. We are a research establishment, and to do our job well, [this](https://x.com/minzlicht/status/2049492221360025765) should be our corporate culture.

[2026-04-30 05:50 UTC] @pranaykotas: This essay argues that good science requires critiquing ideas universally, not shielding "less powerful" scholars from rigorous public criticism.

Michael Inzlicht describes how a norm against "punching down" in academia made senior scholars wary of publicly criticizing students or marginalized researchers, even when their work was weak. He gives examples: a "hot women don't feel cold" paper and a qualitative policing study, where critics were attacked for power dynamics instead of their substantive points. He also skewers "sycophancy down," where average work by marginalized scholars gets exaggerated praise, which he sees as patronizing rather than protective.

He anchors his argument in Merton's norm of universalism: scientific claims should be judged by shared standards, irrespective of who makes them. If we avoid critiquing junior or marginalized scholars, we implicitly treat them as less-than-full participants in science, which is infantilizing. He also notes that "power" is now multi-dimensional: a grad student with a large online following or activist backing can exert real reputational power over a tenured professor, making the punch-up/punch-down frame incoherent.

At the same time, he insists criticism must be delivered with care. Drawing on the Talmudic view that public humiliation is akin to shedding blood, he distinguishes between rigorous critique and personal denigration. The right ideal, in his view, is open, public disagreement about ideas, offered civilly and kindly, with no exemptions from scrutiny based on identity or career stage. Calling for "better fights," he closes by inviting readers to criticize his own argument, regardless of their status.
